channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘The Odyssey’ Review: Passion in Every Frame
item.description: Few directors close the divide between the art film and the blockbuster like Christopher Nolan.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Octavia Spencer Believed in Herself Even When Hollywood Didn’t
item.description: It took years for her to be cast as “the main character,” but she considers that the industry’s loss. Her latest series is the action comedy “Ride or Die.”
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Thea Traff
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I wish I could bottle it up and give it to the world,” Spencer said of her self-confidence, “to say you have importance, take up as much space as you want.”

item.title: Michelangelo’s Forgotten Renaissance Masterpiece
item.description: Tired of the David? A trip across Florence to see the small Santo Spirito Crucifix, a tender vision in old wood, is one of the best decisions you could make in your art-journeying life.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Fine

item.title: Why Did a Major Museum’s Bong Show Go Up in Smoke?
item.description: After the Toledo Museum of Art canceled a pioneering exhibition of glass cannabis pipes, many people wondered what lay behind the decision.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Golbert has been making glass pipes for 30 years.

item.title: ‘Heartstopper Forever’: Growing Up, and Staying True to Themselves
item.description: Like “Heated Rivalry,” this beloved Netflix romance has a big-hearted ethos. It’s bringing that spirit to a new film that rounds out a teenage TV hit.
item.dc:creator: Eleanor Stanford

item.title: On ‘Caribenya,’ Lido Pimienta Found a Lifeline
item.description: On Lido Pimienta’s new album, “Caribenya,” the Colombian Canadian musician sought a warmer, more complex depiction of Caribbean culture. Creating it helped her rediscover the beauty of living.
item.dc:creator: Carolina Abbott Galvão
item.media:credit: Tommy Keith for The New York Times
item.media:description: On Pimienta’s new album, images of dragonflies, rivers and the sea abound, as do depictions of fraught emotional relationships and critiques of colonialism.

item.title: Robert Wilson’s Watermill Center Without Robert Wilson
item.description: “When I’m no longer living, can this place exist?” Robert Wilson wondered in a film about his Long Island art incubator. This month, its annual festival will go on without him.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.media:credit: Yuvraj Khanna for The New York Times
item.media:description: The choreographer Madeline Hollander, center, with Antuan Byers, left, and Cori Kresge at the Watermill Center, where she is creating a response to “Einstein on the Beach.”

item.title: ‘The Potluck’ Review: The Greensboro Massacre, and a Heavy Legacy to Bear
item.description: César Alvarez’s ambitious new work is a mixed musical meal: It wants to be a call to revolutionary action but is surprisingly lacking in it.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: Anthony Alfaro, center, stars in “The Potluck” as César, the stand-in for the show’s playwright, César Alvarez.

item.title: ‘Hadestown’ Hits Movie Theaters, and Theater to Stream in July
item.description: Streaming picks include the family-friendly sci-fi adventure “Franklin’s Key” in Philadelphia and “Hamlet” at the Delacorte Theater in Central Park.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman

item.title: A Beastie Boy Returns to the Mic, With Some Help
item.description: Thanks to an assist from his sons, Mike D is set to release “Thank You,” the first new music from a member of the pioneering rap trio in 15 years.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario and Ryan Lowry
item.media:credit: Ryan Lowry for The New York Times

item.title: T. Rex Fossil Sells for $50.1 Million, Putting the King Back On Top
item.description: In a booming fossil market, the T. rex had been dethroned by a stegosaurus. No longer.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Timothy A. Clary/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The 12-and-a-half-foot-tall T. rex, known as Gus, on view at Sotheby’s ahead of its auction on Tuesday.

item.title: What Happens if Pernambuco Wood Becomes Illegal in Music?
item.description: For centuries, the standard wood used to make string instruments’ bows has come from the pernambuco tree in Brazil. Its future is uncertain.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Reynolds and Vincent Tullo
item.category: Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species
item.media:description: Bows are generally made from pernambuco wood; some older ones even include materials like ivory.

item.title: As White House Leans on Smithsonian, Little Uproar Over Board Vacancies
item.description: An odd silence has surrounded a delay to fill several open seats on the board of the Smithsonian, even as a fierce battle to control the institution ensues.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:description: For more than a year, President Trump has attacked the Smithsonian Institution. Typically, Congress would have confirmed new board nominees by now.

item.title: With Lizzo and Calm Flair, a Storied Venue Reopens for Summer
item.description: The gorgeously renovated Hunter Pavilion at Ravinia, the Chicago Symphony Orchestra’s off-season home, was inaugurated with a gala last weekend.
item.dc:creator: David Allen
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lizzo performing with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra at Ravinia on Saturday.

item.title: Jimmy Fallon Pokes Fun at Joe Biden’s New Memoir
item.description: “It’s the second book Biden has written,” Fallon said of the former president. “The first was ‘The Odyssey.’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: PEN America Defends Israel Article That Led to President’s Resignation
item.description: A co-chief executive of the free expression group said a recent article about Israeli and Jewish writers had been “critical” to pursue.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.media:credit: Cindy Ord/Getty Images For Pen America
item.media:description: Clarisse Rosaz Shariyf, left, and Summer Lopez were named co-chief executives of PEN America in February.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Some Delight to Your Day
item.description: Odysseus’ social network, performative boredom and decorated envelopes.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Scott Bryce, ‘As the World Turns’ Actor, Dies at 68
item.description: He originated the role of the scheming Craig Montgomery and appeared in 280 episodes of the CBS soap opera over more than two decades.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz
item.media:description: Scott Bryce in 2007. He originated the role of Craig Montgomery on “As the World Turns” in 1982 and played him off and on until 2008.

item.title: For the Eclipse or a Weekend, How to Save Money in Reykjavik
item.description: A museum pass, public hot springs and free airline stopovers help shrink the cost of visiting Iceland’s capital.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Glusac and Sigga Ella
item.media:credit: Sigga Ella for The New York Times

item.title: Late Night Calls Lindsey Graham’s Senate Seat ‘an Inheritance Jackpot’
item.description: “A Senate seat? Most people just get a garage full of damp boxes,” Michael Kosta said of Graham’s sister filling the late senator’s seat on “The Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: A Soundtrack to the 2026 World Cup Semifinals
item.description: Listen to songs from Rosalía, Mauvais Djo, Ca7riel & Paco Amoroso and the Beatles.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:description: Fans of the Argentina team celebrating after a win over Switzerland in the World Cup quarterfinals.

item.title: ‘The Whoopi Monologues’ Review: Expanding a One-Woman Show
item.description: Instead of one Whoopi Goldberg, audiences now experience five actresses: Kerry Washington, Kara Young, Dominique Fishback, Danielle Pinnock and Kecia Lewis.
item.dc:creator: Brittani Samuel
item.media:description: From left: Kecia Lewis, Dominique Fishback, Danielle Pinnock, Kerry Washington and Kara Young in the play “The Whoopi Monologues” at the Mitzi E. Newhouse Theater.

item.title: At Young World Festival, Rap’s Avant-Garde Takes Shape
item.description: The Brooklyn festival Young World spotlights emergent indie rappers and veteran artists. It was the brainchild of Mike, an artist and label head who has become a fulcrum of the alt-rap scene.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Green and Marcus Maddox
item.media:credit: Marcus Maddox for The New York Times

item.title: In ‘The Westies,’ Chris Brancato Expands His ‘Scumbag Universe’ (Again)
item.description: For his new series, the co-creator of “Narcos” and “Godfather of Harlem” turns his focus to a violent gang of Irish New Yorkers in the early 1980s.
item.dc:creator: Austin Considine and David Billet
item.media:credit: David Billet for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Difficult People’ a Decade Later: A Sarcastic Time Capsule
item.description: This cult comedy about two snide show-business outsiders has a new Blu-ray edition, and a reunion this fall. The creator Julie Klausner discusses why she’d love to revive it.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Egner
item.media:description: Julie Klausner and Billy Eichner starred in “Difficult People,” which ran for three seasons on Hulu. (With Derrick Baskin, background.)

item.title: Jon Stewart Mocks Mitch McConnell’s Recovery Photo
item.description: The “Daily Show” host called the image “totally believable evidence that Mitch McConnell is not only alive, but the happiest boy in the hospital.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Pat Oliphant, Cartoonist Who Skewered the Powerful, Dies at 90
item.description: The winner of countless awards, including a Pulitzer Prize, he was described in 1990 as “the most influential political cartoonist now working.”
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.media:description: Pat Oliphant at his studio in Washington in 1998. “If Pat Oliphant couldn’t draw, he’d be an assassin,” one critic observed of his acid-dipped pen.

item.title: Sam Neill’s Best Movie Performances: Where to Stream
item.description: From his global breakthrough in “Jurassic Park” to the more recent “Peaky Blinders,” the actor’s range was always on display.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: Tony Rayns, Who Helped Bring Asian Film to the World, Dies at 77
item.description: In reviews and at festivals, he played a key role in introducing English-speaking audiences to directors like Wong Kar-wai, Jia Zhangke and Bong Joon Ho.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: Labels for Music Created With A.I. Could Join Explicit Lyrics Warnings
item.description: Major music industry groups, including the organization behind the Grammy Awards, have proposed labels for tracks made with some degree of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.category: International Federation of the Phonographic Industry

item.title: Paramount-Warner Bros. Merger Is Challenged in Court by California and 11 Other States
item.description: The lawsuit, filed by California and 11 other states, argues that the deal would harm movie theaters and damage America’s entertainment industry.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin, David McCabe and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paramount’s acquisition of Warner Bros. was scheduled to be completed in the third quarter this year. Above, Warner Bros. in Burbank, Calif.

item.title: Shahrnush Parsipur, Iranian Writer Imprisoned for Her Novels, Dies at 80
item.description: Her best-known work, “Women Without Men,” was longlisted for the International Booker Prize this year.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Shahrnush Parsipur in 2009. Her novels and stories about women in Iran before and after the Islamic Revolution made her one of the country’s most acclaimed writers of her generation.

item.title: Jay-Z Yankee Stadium Review: Three Nights of Legacy and Guest Stars
item.description: The rap mogul convened his starriest collaborators, showed off his still-dazzling skills and made a convincing argument for his singular legacy.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica and Lexi Parra

item.title: A Murder Mystery Evening at Audrey Gelman’s Guesthouse
item.description: At her Hudson Valley guesthouse, Audrey Gelman, a co-founder of the Wing, channels her childlike love of play with murder mystery evenings.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Guests are encouraged to “dress to impress” for the dinner and murder mystery evening.

item.title: Sizzling, Sexy Age-Gap Romance Novels
item.description: The best-selling author Rachel Lynn Solomon recommends forbidden love stories full of fiery desire, shifting power dynamics and so-wrong-it’s-right drama.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Lynn Solomon
item.media:credit: The New York Times; Author photo by Sabreen Lakhani

item.title: Sam Neill, New Zealand Actor Known for ‘Jurassic Park,’ Dies at 78
item.description: He appeared in more than 150 films and TV shows over a five-decade career, but was perhaps best known for his star turn in the dinosaur blockbuster.
item.dc:creator: Natasha Frost and Zachary Woolfe
item.media:credit: Nick Haddow/Penske Media via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mr. Neill in 1995, the year he directed “Cinema of Unease,” a documentary about New Zealand cinema.

item.title: ‘Lucky’ and 6 More Shows to Watch on TV this Week
item.description: A new crime thriller comes to Apple TV. And Sean Evans hosts “Hot Ones: Extra Heat.”
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: Anya Taylor-Joy in “Lucky.”

item.title: ‘House of the Dragon’ Season 3, Episode 4 Recap: Heavy Is the Head
item.description: In her attempts to establish order, Rhaenyra is already alienating some of her most vital allies, including the Sea Snake.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:description: Corlys (Steve Toussaint) left King’s Landing to battle pirates on the sea. It isn’t the most obvious mission for a hand of the queen to undertake.

item.title: Why Dior’s Jonathan Anderson Is Inspired by Lynda Benglis
item.description: Lynda Benglis redefined sculpture in the 1960s. No longer overlooked, her audacious talent moves from museums to runways, transformed by Dior’s Jonathan Anderson.
item.dc:creator: Julia Halperin

item.title: ‘Moana’ Falters at the Box Office, Casting Doubt on Disney’s Formula
item.description: The live-action remake took in roughly $43 million over the weekend in North America, about 30 percent less than Disney had expected.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:description: Dwayne Johnson as Maui in “Moana,” which cost an estimated $250 million to make and at least $100 million to market worldwide.

item.title: Melbourne Symphony Wins Discrimination Case Over Gaza Comments
item.description: The pianist Jayson Gillham had sued the orchestra after it canceled his performance in the wake of comments he made against Israel’s war with Hamas.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: The classical pianist Jayson Gillham outside federal court in Melbourne, Australia, in May.

item.title: Fans of ‘Love Island USA’ Ask: ‘Are These Couples for Real?’
item.description: For many viewers of the hit reality TV show, lively debates over whether the competing couples are truly in love is a central part of being a fan.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs

item.title: ‘Michael’ Crosses $1 Billion at the Box Office as Lionsgate Roars Back
item.description: Shunned by bigger studios as too risky, “Michael” and other hits have revived Hollywood’s last major independent studio. But is it enough?
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: Don’t Try This at Home: How Performers Turn Stunts Into Movie Magic
item.description: At the International Stunt School in Seattle, veterans of TV and film teach newcomers the art of falls, fights and fire.
item.dc:creator: Chris Maggio and Erynn Lamont
item.media:credit: Chris Maggio for The New York Times

item.title: Tracee Ellis Ross Is Back in New York City, Living a Dream Come True
item.description: The actress and entrepreneur got an apartment in the city for the summer while she makes her Broadway debut in “Every Brilliant Thing.”
item.dc:creator: Addie Morfoot
item.media:credit: Sabrina Santiago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tracee Ellis Ross is in New York City for three months as she makes her Broadway debut. She stopped by Health & Harmony in Greenwich Village for some groceries.

item.title: Beyoncé, the Rolling Stones: Songs to Know This Week
item.description: Beyoncé gets amorous, the Rolling Stones stay loud and the Linda Lindas team up with Hayley Williams.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: The Rolling Stones deliver straightforward, propulsive rock on their new album.

item.title: Whistle-Blowers Accuse Kennedy Center of Contracting Flaws Under Trump
item.description: Documents submitted to Congress detailed concerns about competitive bidding processes and a White House order to tear up new bathroom tile because of its color.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Zach Montague
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: Since President Trump took over the Kennedy Center, he helped secure $257 million from Congress to fund building renovation and maintenance. The center’s contracting is now under scrutiny.

item.title: Yaacov Agam, Who Made Kinetic, Kaleidoscopic Art, Dies at 98
item.description: An Israeli artist, he became a global sensation, creating sculptures, paintings, toys and public works that delighted the eye and confounded perception.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:description: Yaacov Agam in 2003 with one of his pieces at the Pompidou Center in Paris. He described his work as “the plastic expression of Einstein’s space‐time continuum.”

item.title: How Good Is Jon Hamm at Playing Jon Hamm?
item.description: Inside the high-wire act of casting celebrities to play themselves, including in movies like “Gail Daughtry and the Celebrity Sex Pass.”
item.dc:creator: Matthew Huff
item.category: The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent (Movie)
Gail Daughtry and the Celebrity Sex Pass (Movie)

item.title: Orna Guralnik of ‘Couples Therapy’ Considers Kendrick Lamar a Genius
item.description: Outside of her job as the therapist on the series “Couples Therapy,” she has a favorite clip of the rapper performing and shows it to everyone she knows.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times

item.title: Our Insatiable Appetite for Stories About Cannibalism
item.description: Why do the characters in so many new novels insist upon eating each other?
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: Kids Can’t Stop Watching ‘Moana.’ There’s a Scientific Explanation.
item.description: The 2016 release has become the most watched movie on Disney+. Parents and experts explain why kids can’t get enough.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: Streams of “Moana” on Disney+ total more than 1.5 billion hours.

item.title: ‘Giulia’ Review: Jennifer Nettles Delivers Empowerment (and Poison)
item.description: A stirring but tonally muddled new musical about the “poison queen of Palermo” gets an elegant Off Broadway production.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: Giulia: The Poison Queen of Palermo (Play)
Perelman, Ronald O, Performing Arts Center
item.media:credit: Richard Termine for The New York Times

item.title: Historian Kerri K. Greenidge and Her Book ‘The Grimkes’ Come Under Fire
item.description: Kerri K. Greenidge appeared to lose her professorship at Tufts University after scholars began scrutinizing her 2022 book, “The Grimkes,” which is no longer listed on its publisher’s website.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.category: Black Radical: The Life and Times of William Monroe Trotter (Book)
item.media:credit: Mark Elzey Jr for The New York Times
item.media:description: Historians are questioning the validity of multiple works by the historian Kerri K. Greenidge, who is no longer employed by Tufts University.

item.title: Museum Spreads 800 Pounds of Peanut Butter in Tribute to Dutch Artist
item.description: An installation honoring the artist, Wim T. Schippers, features a 270-square-foot hexagon spread on the floor of a museum in Rotterdam.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: Workers at the Museum Boijmans Van Beuningen in Rotterdam, the Netherlands, recreated “Peanut Butter Floor,” an art installation by Wim T. Schippers, last week.

item.title: Review: ‘Suddenly Last Summer’ Opera Premieres at Bard
item.description: Tennessee Williams’s darkly operatic one-act play becomes a proper opera in a new adaptation by the composer Courtney Bryan.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.category: Fisher, Richard B, Center for the Performing Arts at Bard College

item.title: Five Action Movies to Stream Now
item.description: Upgrade your movie night with these picks, featuring karate-chopping families, rogue assassins and more.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels

item.title: 7 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: Gail Daughtry and the Celebrity Sex Pass (Movie)

item.title: Hershel Parker, Melville Scholar of Ahab-Like Obsessiveness, Dies at 90
item.description: The author of an exhaustive two-volume biography, he was “fanatical about his approach to scholarship and infatuated with Melville,” a colleague said.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: Dinaw Mengestu Resigns as PEN America President After Israel Article
item.description: The novelist Dinaw Mengestu, who was elected seven months ago, said the article “continues this approach toward defending some rights while not defending others.”
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.media:description: Dinaw Mengestu had been on PEN America’s board for about a decade before he became its president in December.

item.title: Michelle Buteau and the Surprising Transgression of “Survival of the Thickest”
item.description: The Netflix rom-com series that just ended after three seasons succeeded thanks to an irresistible performance by its star, Michelle Buteau.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips
item.media:description: Michelle Buteau in the third season of Netflix’s “Survival of the Thickest.”

item.title: Book Review: ‘A Vast Horizon,’ by Anna Thomasson
item.description: A group of artists gathered at a hotel on the Côte d’Azur in 1937. A new book by Anna Thomasson captures the art and escapades the holiday inspired.
item.dc:creator: Emily Eakin
item.category: A Vast Horizon: Artists and Lovers, Freedom and War (Book)
item.media:description: Pablo Picasso and Dora Maar at Golfe-Juan, France, on the Côte d’Azur in 1937.
